questions.q: In Hindu thought, what does the concept of dharma most broadly refer to?
options: The god of wealth and prosperity
A sacred river of the Himalayas
The cosmic order and righteous duty that sustains creation
questions.explanation: Dharma is the cosmic order and the duty proper to each being that upholds creation, as expounded across the Vedas, Dharmashastras, and the epics.

questions.q: The word dharma derives from the Sanskrit root 'dhr', which means what?
questions.explanation: Dharma comes from the root dhr, meaning to hold or sustain, reflecting its role in keeping creation from collapse.

questions.q: Which animal is the enduring symbol traditionally associated with dharma in Hindu tradition?
questions.explanation: Dharma is often personified, and the bull is its enduring symbol in the tradition.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, on how many legs does Dharma, personified, stand fully in the Satya Yuga?
questions.explanation: The Puranas teach that Dharma stands on four legs in the Satya Yuga, losing one leg in each successive age.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, which prince of Ayodhya is revered as Maryada Purushottama, the perfect embodiment of dharma?
questions.explanation: Rama, hero of Valmiki's Ramayana, is revered as Maryada Purushottama, the perfect embodiment of dharma.

questions.q: Which deity of the Trimurti is described as preserving cosmic order, or dharma, descending as avatars whenever it declines?
questions.explanation: Vishnu the preserver sustains cosmic order (dharma) and descends as the avatars whenever it falters.

questions.q: Which of these is one of the four classical aims of human life (purusharthas) in Hindu tradition, alongside dharma?
questions.explanation: The four purusharthas are dharma, artha, kama, and moksha, the last being final liberation of the soul.

questions.q: As dharma declines across the four yugas, which age is described in the Puranas as the present, darkest age?
questions.explanation: The present Kali Yuga is the darkest age, in which dharma is reduced to a single leg.

questions.q: Dharma is expounded across many texts; which of the following is a Dharmashastra dealing with law and duty?
questions.explanation: The Manusmriti is among the Dharmashastras that expound dharma, the duty proper to each being.

questions.q: The future avatar Kalki is prophesied in the Puranas to appear at the end of Kali Yuga to accomplish what regarding dharma?
options: To renew dharma and usher in a new Satya Yuga
To hide the Vedas beneath the ocean
questions.explanation: Kalki is said to come to destroy the wicked, end the age of darkness, and usher in the renewal of dharma and a new Satya Yuga.

questions.q: In the Bhagavad Gita, what does Krishna declare he descends to accomplish whenever dharma declines?
options: To churn the ocean of milk
To establish dharma and protect the righteous
To bring the Ganga down to earth
questions.explanation: In the Gita, Krishna says he takes birth in age after age to protect the good, destroy the wicked, and re-establish dharma.

questions.q: Which classical text genre, including the Manusmriti, specifically codifies dharma as law and duty?
questions.explanation: The Dharmashastras, such as the Manusmriti, are the texts that codify dharma as law and duty.

questions.q: Which deity, the impartial judge of the dead, is also titled Dharmaraja, lord of righteousness?
questions.explanation: Yama, king of the dead and impartial judge of souls, is also Dharmaraja, the lord of righteousness.

questions.q: The four yugas form a complete cycle across which dharma steadily diminishes. What is this set of four ages collectively called?
questions.explanation: Satya, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali together form a chaturyuga, across which dharma declines from four legs to one.

questions.q: In the Vedas, which term denotes the cosmic and moral order closely connected to the idea of dharma, upheld by the god Varuna?
questions.explanation: Rita is the Vedic cosmic and moral order upheld by Varuna, an early conception kindred to dharma.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, the righteous brother of Ravana who urges him to uphold dharma and return Sita is whom?
questions.explanation: Vibhishana counsels Ravana to return Sita and uphold dharma, and is later consecrated king of Lanka by Rama.

questions.q: The four purusharthas place dharma first among life's aims. What does the term artha denote among them?
questions.explanation: Among the purusharthas, artha denotes material prosperity and worldly success, pursued in harmony with dharma.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the present Kali Yuga, the age in which dharma is weakest, is said to have begun at the close of whose earthly life?
questions.explanation: The Puranas say the Kali Yuga began at the close of Krishna's earthly life, the age in which dharma is reduced to one leg.

questions.q: The oldest layer of scripture in which the conception of cosmic order underlying dharma first appears is which body of texts?
questions.explanation: Dharma is expounded across the Vedas, the Dharmashastras, and the epics, the Vedas being the oldest scriptural layer.

questions.q: Which goddess of knowledge and speech, consort of the creator Brahma, embodies the wisdom by which dharma is learned and transmitted?
questions.explanation: Saraswati is the goddess of knowledge, speech, and the arts, consort of Brahma and embodiment of learning.

questions.q: In the Ramayana, Rama upholds his father Dasharatha's promise by accepting what, a celebrated example of dharma?
options: A fourteen-year exile to the forest
questions.explanation: Rama accepts a fourteen-year forest exile to honor his father's word, a celebrated act of upholding dharma.

questions.q: The Bhagavad Gita teaches a doctrine concerning one's own dharma. What is the term for a person's individual duty according to their nature and station?
questions.explanation: Sva-dharma is one's own individual duty according to nature and station, central to the Gita's teaching to Arjuna.

questions.q: In the Mahabharata, the eldest Pandava Yudhishthira is so devoted to truth and duty that he bears which epithet related to dharma?
questions.explanation: Yudhishthira, born of the god Dharma, is called Dharmaputra and Dharmaraja for his unwavering adherence to righteousness.

questions.q: In the Mahabharata, a famous trial of dharma occurs at the Yaksha Prashna, where a yaksha questions a Pandava beside a lake. The yaksha is later revealed to be which deity?
options: Yama, in the form of Dharma
questions.explanation: In the Yaksha Prashna, the questioning yaksha is revealed to be Yama, the god Dharma, testing his son Yudhishthira.

questions.q: At the close of the Mahabharata, a faithful dog accompanies Yudhishthira on his final ascent and is revealed to be which divinity in disguise?
questions.explanation: The dog that follows Yudhishthira to heaven is the god Dharma himself, testing his compassion and steadfastness.

questions.q: Across the four yugas, by how many legs does Dharma, personified as the bull, diminish from one age to the next?
options: By half a leg per age
questions.explanation: Dharma stands on four legs in the Satya Yuga and loses one leg in each successive age until only one remains in Kali Yuga.

questions.q: Hindu tradition speaks of Sanatana Dharma. What does the term 'sanatana' signify in this phrase?
questions.explanation: Sanatana means eternal or everlasting, and Sanatana Dharma denotes the eternal order or law.

questions.q: In Hindu tradition, the duties that are common to all beings, such as non-violence and truthfulness, regardless of caste or stage of life, are termed what?
questions.explanation: Sadharana or samanya dharma refers to the universal duties common to all, such as truthfulness and non-violence.

questions.q: The framework relating dharma to one's caste (varna) and stage of life (ashrama) is known by what compound term?
questions.explanation: Varnashrama dharma is the framework prescribing duties according to one's varna (caste) and ashrama (stage of life).

questions.q: In the Mahabharata, the duty prescribed for one facing extreme distress or emergency, when ordinary rules of dharma may be set aside, is termed what?
questions.explanation: Apad-dharma is the dharma applicable in times of distress or emergency, discussed at length in the Mahabharata's Shanti Parva.
